nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; juveniles favor shallow turbid inshore waters and sand slopes, while adults move to deeper protected bays, lagoons, and outer reef areas down to roughly 90 m.

decorPreferences: large amounts of live rock for grazing and refuge
long footprint preferred over deep footprint

diet.notes: In the wild, the Yellowfin Tang primarily grazes filamentous algae, diatoms, and detrital film, but is unusual among surgeonfishes in also taking small invertebrates and even pieces of fish. In captivity, dried marine seaweed or nori is generally advised three or more times per week, alongside spirulina-based prepared foods; meaty items such as mysis or krill can be offered occasionally. A diet with a strong algae component tends to support immune health and reduce the risk of HLLE.

avoidWith: other Acanthurus tangs in undersized tanks
conspecifics (unless tank exceeds several thousand liters)
small timid fish that may be bullied

summary: The Yellowfin Tang is one of the largest members of the genus Acanthurus, capable of reaching 50 to 70 cm as an adult. It is suited to very large reef or fish-only systems with long open swim lanes and a steady supply of marine algae.
careNotes: The Yellowfin Tang (Acanthurus xanthopterus) is one of the largest members of its genus, with adults commonly reaching 50 cm and exceptional individuals approaching 70 cm. Juveniles display bright yellow pectoral and anal fins on a yellow-grey body; as they mature, the coloration shifts to a more uniform dusky purple-brown, with yellow retained as a pectoral patch and a distinctive mask around the eyes — the feature behind the alternate trade name Yellow-Mask Surgeonfish. Like all tangs, the Yellowfin possesses a sharp, scalpel-like spine on either side of the caudal peduncle (the narrow base of the tail) that can inflict serious cuts on handlers and tankmates alike; caution is warranted whenever the fish is moved or netted. Tangs in this genus are commonly considered susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition in which pitting and discoloration develop along the head and along the sensory line running down the body, typically linked to poor water quality or a diet lacking vitamin-rich marine algae. Frequent water changes and a diet with a strong algae component are generally advised to minimize this risk. The Yellowfin Tang is broadly regarded as reef-safe — adults are not known to nip at corals — though their considerable size means unsecured coral frags may be dislodged by accidental contact.
breedingNotes: Has not been reliably bred in home aquaria. Spawning in the wild occurs in pelagic aggregations with broadcast release of eggs and a long planktonic larval phase, which is impractical to replicate without dedicated larval-rearing infrastructure.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy, native range, wild temperature range (24-28 C), max size (70 cm TL), lifespan (up to 34 yr in the wild), and diet ecology.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~50 cm) and minimum tank volume (1800 L). Higher tank-size recommendation than LiveAquaria; averaging biased toward the larger figure per the tank-sizing rule, then rounded to 1500 L.
